Now that we've got a complete ecosystem of components for combat, we'll  
expand upon those things to add more features, quality, and scripting  
technique to our game. We'll polish the feel of the player attack, the  
TFB Tool  
Handbook  
projectile, and the enemy attack, set up a hit point system and have the  
enemy killable, create an enemy spawner, and learn about how we can  
generalize our scripts to allow for maximum ﬂexibility and power. In this  
secion, you'll learn about:

Polishing the Enemy's Attack  
Before actually adding more stuﬀ to our level, it's worth taking a moment to do a little polishing work and expose  
some of the attendant features that will be useful down the road. Right now, something that bothers me about the  
enemy is that he will attack you repeatedly without any delay. It makes him a very uninteresting enemy since you  
have almost no window of opportunity to strike back at him.  
The Script Timer and Arithmetic  
When the enemy ﬁnishes his attack, I'd like to have the enemy delay a bit before attacking again. The easiest way to  
build a delay is to use the Script Timer: a built-in system variable that exists in every script and constantly tells  
you the time that script has been active.  
1 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Add a new print command to the prescript of the enemy and print the (SYSTEM) > Script Timer to target.  
Live update and take a look at what's being printed on-screen. You'll notice a number that's constantly ascending -  
it's the time, in seconds, since the script started up. Since you know the Script Timer at any given frame when a  
command executes, you can set a value variable to the current value of the Script Timer plus the amount of time  
you want to delay, then, later, check to see if the Script Timer has surpassed the value.  
Create a new value variable in the STARTUP of the enemy script, and name it attack delay timer.  
In the attack behavior, add a new set value command when we return to the chase behavior.  
Set the lefthand side to be the local attack delay timer value, and set the righthand side to be the (SYSTEM)  
> Script Timer value.  
Left-click on the righthand side again, and select the + menu option to add a plus sign.  
A new ﬁeld will appear on the righthand side, allowing you to specify another value.  
Left-click it, and set it to 0.5, with the intent that the enemy will wait half a second before attacking again.  
Now, in the section of the chase behavior where we change to the attack behavior, add a new check value  
command and check if the Script Timer is greater than or equal to (>=) the attack delay timer value you  
just set.  
2 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Finally, Ctrl+Click all the lines for changing behavior and playing the animation to select them.  
Then place them in the new Script Timer check.  
Now, even if the enemy has moved close enough to the player in the chase behavior, if 0.5 seconds hasn't passed  
since his last attack, he won't attack again. If you're confused about how/why the timer logic we just did works,  
think of it this way:  
Let's say the Script Timer is at 30.000 (30 seconds) when we're leaving the attack behavior.  
If we set a local value to the Script Timer plus 0.5, the local value will now be 30.5.  
The Script Timer will count up in real time, taking 0.5 seconds to surpass the 30.5 we stored in our local  
value.  
Go ahead and live update to check out the enemy's newly improved behavior. You may decide for yourself that 0.5  
seconds is too short or long of a delay, but it's easily tuned by playing with the value in the set value command we  
added!  
You'll notice that, while the enemy does, indeed, delay his attack, he keeps playing his run animation. That is an  
unfortunate side-eﬀect of how we set up the run animation to play as part of the move to, but we'll come back to  
resolve that later.  
Polishing the Player's Attack  
Now that the enemy has a touch of class and ﬁghts like a gentleman, it would be nice to clean up some of the  
player's feel issues as well. We'll make his projectile feel a little more deadly, add some auto-aiming logic so it's  
easier to shoot the enemy, and, ﬁnally, speed up his attack animation so he can attack more frequently.  
Projectile Tweaks  
While the novelty of our gently lobbed projectile was amusing for a while, now that we have the threat of an  
attacking enemy (with a spear, no less!), it would be nice to revisit the projectile for some enhancements. Rather  
than being an upward lob, I'd prefer it to be more like a bullet, ﬁring straight ahead with a decent velocity.  
3 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Tweak the projectile's movement properties so that it has 0 gravity max speed and accel, which will cause it to  
never accelerate downwards because of gravity.  
If you were to live update and ﬁre the projectile, it would soar oﬀ into the air - it would actually never come back  
down since it has no gravity! Let's adjust the displace line that launches the projectile so that it doesn't have any  
upward momentum.  
Open the player's script and set the displace line when we ﬁre the projectile to have 0 pitch.  
If you were to ﬁre the projectile right now, you'd note that nothing will actually show up. That's because the  
projectile is detecting that it's hitting the ground. Since this projectile is now designed to ﬂy straight ahead, we'll  
use the other function of the displace command to lift it up oﬀ the ground.  
Right before the displace on the projectile's velocity, add a new displace line, but set it to displace the  
^cloned projectile's <self>, as opposed to velocity.  
The diﬀerence between displacing an object (<self>) versus displacing velocity, is that displacing an object is  
eﬀectively going to teleport it, much like we did by setting the distance value with our camera script. Displacing  
velocity adjusts the speed of an object, wheras displacing the object, itself, adjusts the location directly.  
Adjust the displace to move 6 feet, with a heading of 0 and pitch of 90 degrees. Eﬀectively, 6 feet straight up  
from where the projectile is spawning (on the ground where the player is).  
Live update and try shooting some bullets, maybe even at the enemy. Ha ha, right in the eye, now. TAKE THAT! One  
last thing to improve our bullet's feel, thuogh. Our bullet is as slow as a limp-legged dog in molasses, so let's speed  
up the displace line on the projectile's velocity to, say, 80 feet.  
Set the velocity displace's length to 80 feet.  
Now you should be able to ﬁre a fairly fast-moving bullet. The only problem is in the projectile's logic for when it  
should be destroyed. It will either be removed when it touches the ground, which has become rather unlikely since  
it no longer has gravity, or when it hits the enemy. If you miss the enemy with your shot, the bullet will ﬂy  
indeﬁnitely. You may be noticing on your TeraTerm log (or xbWatson if on the 360) a bunch of spam saying the  
projectile's out of the world. That is, indeed, a bad thing. Let's add some logic so the projectile does get cleaned up  
naturally if it travels too far from its spawn point.  
Open the projectile's script and a new check value line to the projectile and set it to check if the distance  
from its origin is greater than 200 feet.  
4 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Add a new remove command to have the projectile remove itself if the check is true.  
Since the projectile is being spawned, its origin is wherever it is being spawned. When it gets beyond 200 feet from  
where it's been spawned, it will remove itself.  
Autoaiming with Check FoV  
Right now, targeting the enemy requires very precise aim. You have to be facing almost perfectly towards the enemy  
before you ﬁre. It would be useful if we could add some leniency and autoaim for the player if he's facing in  
generally the right direction. We can do this with the check fov command, which is short for check ﬁeld of view,  
that lets us project a ﬂattened cone outwards to look for something.  
In the player's script, right inside the clone of the projectile, add a new check fov line.  
There are several parameters for a check fov, so let's list out what each one means:  
from: Object where the fov check will origin.  
facing: Angle of the center of the cone - what direction will we be projecting it?  
covering: Angle of the cone's width. If you looked for something within 90 degrees, it would check 45 degrees  
to both the left and the right.  
for: What should the check fov be searching for?  
= n distance: What are the distance rules for the check? For example, you could check if something was  
closer than 5 feet in the cone, or greater than 20 feet away.  
Let's set up the check fov to look for the enemy from the player within a reasonable angle/distance.  
Set check fov's parameters up as follows: from myself facing my facing covering 90 degrees for Enemy  
Actor less than or equal to 60 feet.  
The check fov is just like our other conditional checks, in that it can succeed or fail - we may be looking at the  
enemy or may not be. In the case where we're not looking at the enemy, we still want to launch the projectile, but if  
the check succeeds, let's launch the projectile directly at the enemy.  
Start by adding an else case to the check fov.  
Drag the displace velocity into the else section.  
5 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Copy and paste the displace velocity line into the true section.  
If the check succeeds, that means the enemy is within the fov's cone. Check fov is one of a few checks that can  
check a [set] of multiple objects, and it returns a scoped variable called ^[fov] which includes all the objects found  
by the check. In this case, we're only looking for one object, the enemy, so we'd only expect one thing to come back.  
We'll talk more about using sets, shortly, but, for now, let's ﬁre the projectile toward the ﬁrst member of that set.  
Adjust the heading of the displace velocity in the true case to be the ^[fov] > ﬁrst > heading from myself.  
When you're done, the check fov block should look like this.  
Live update and check out how the projectile behaves. You should notice that when you're facing the approximate  
direction of the enemy, it'll shoot the bullet directly at him. If you're using the arrow model I chose earlier, you may  
also notice that the bullet's facing is no longer representative of the bullet's velocity. We can ﬁx that by setting the  
facing after we set the velocity.  
Add a new set value line after the whole check fov block, and set the projectile's facing to be its velocity's >  
heading.  
6 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Now the bullet's facing will match the way it's being launched.  
Animation Manipulation  
The ﬁnal bit of tweaking I'd like to do has to do with the player's attack animation. I'm sure you've noticed that it's  
really, really long. We can't attack very quickly, especially when we compare the player's attack rate to the enemy's.  
Though we could select another animation, I wanted to show some script power by speeding up the animation to  
make it complete faster.  
In the player's script, add a new set value command to the play block where the attack animation is being  
played.  
Adjust the set value to set ^playing's > playback rate to 300 percent.  
Live update, and the animation will now play at triple speed. Much better!  
Tracking Hit Points  
Now that we've tuned our player and enemy a bit, let's add some real consequence to the combat - at least for the  
enemy - by letting the player kill him when he runs out of hit points. I'm sure you're noticing a theme in the tool:  
any gameplay features we want, we add ourselves. We built a camera system of sorts, we've constructed behaviors  
for the player, projectile, and enemy. Hit points will be the same: they only are what we make them to be. That is,  
there's no inherent concept of hit points in our tool/system. We're going to make it all up! I'm going to let you oﬀ  
the leash again to think about how you'd want to do this, but the general design will be:  
Creating a hit points value variable in the enemy's script and initializing it to some number.  
Deducting hit points when the enemy takes a hit.  
Killing the enemy when he runs out of hit points.  
I'll show you my solution in a moment, but why don't you try implementing this, yourself? At the very least, take a  
bit to think about how you'd implement it before proceeding.  
Did you think about it?  
How would you do it?  
Think you've got it worked out, maybe even working in the game?  
There's no going back after this!  
Implementing HP for the Enemy  
7 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Before I talk about my implementation steps, something important to me was to keep the hit points very localized  
in the enemy. That is, I could have the projectile, when setting the hurt value on collision with the enemy, deduct  
some amount of hit points, I thought it would be best to keep it all in the enemy as much as possible. Now, with  
that in mind, here are the steps I took.  
I created a hp (abbreviation for 'hit points') value variable in the enemy.  
In the enemy's startup, I initialize the hp value to an above-zero number (I used 3).  
When the enemy was hurt, I used a new command, dec value, on the hp value. This is synonymous with  
setting hp to hp - 1, it's just a nice shortcut. Though I'm not using it here, another shortcut command, inc  
value adds 1 to the value.  
The actual hurt block looked like this:  
Finally, I added a print to target so I could see how many hp the enemy has at any given moment.  
If you're following along, you should be able to build and launch, shoot the enemy, and see his HP drop.  
Killing the Enemy  
The only issue is that his hp can become negative! When it reaches 0, he really should die, which, for me, meant  
playing a death animation, then removing him afterward.  
I started by creating a *death animation reference, so I could track the progress of his death animation.  
Then, in his hurt check, I check to see if his hp have reached (or gone below, just for safety) 0 after  
decrementing the hp value.  
If the enemy did run out of hp, I want him to change to a new behavior. I made a new behavior, death, for him  
to go to.  
8 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Since I didn't want the set behavior command right below to override and send him to the hurt behavior, I  
changed my check value to have an else and moved the regular hurt logic in there.  
I looked in the Model Editor and found a good death animation in slot 6, so I made him play it and set my  
*death animation reference to the playing animation.  
Now, in the newly-added death behavior, I check to see if the *death animation is complete. If it is, I remove  
myself.  
If you're following along, live update, and try out the newly improved, mortality-respecting enemy! You'll notice that  
when you hurt him and his hp hits 0, he'll play his death animation and vanish, shortly thereafter. For reference, an  
image of the ﬁnal script at this point is below.  
9 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
NOTE: If you kill him and want him to come back, just check his Template box and uncheck it to force him to  
restart.  
Fading the Enemy Out  
The enemy dies rather abruptly right now. He plays his death animation, but once the animation is done, he just  
pops out. I'd like to add a fade to the enemy, and I'll accomplish that by adjusting his tint color with a slide value.  
Start by adding a new behavior called death fade, then, when the death animation completes, instead of  
removing himself, having the enemy change to that behavior.  
Now we'll use two new concepts at once. The most important one to understand is the slide value command, which  
is just a set value that occurs over time. You specify the value you wish to change, the target for the value, and  
how long it should take for the transition to occur.  
Add a new slide value command in the block where we change to the death fade behavior.  
10 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
The second concept we'll utilize is the tint. Every actor (and particle and sprite) has a tint, which is comprised of  
Red, Green, Blue, and Alpha channels that range from 0 (black for RGB, invisible for Alpha) to 255 (fully tinted for  
RGB, fully visible for Alpha). In this case, we'll just be sliding the alpha value to cause a fade eﬀect.  
Pull down on the lefthand side of the slide value and select my > tint's > alpha.  
The default target value of the slide is 0, which is what we want (alpha to 0), so now we just need to specify a time.  
Set the over __ seconds parameter to 0.2, for 1/5th of a second.  
Now, if you try updating and killing the enemy, you'll see him play his death animation, then fade out over a brief  
period. However, remember that we took away the remove parameter. Even though the enemy has, indeed, faded  
out, he's not removing and cleaning himself up. If you have some prints in his script, you'll see they're still printing.  
We can't add a remove command in the death fade behavior, since it would execute right away. Just like with our  
animations, we need a method of hanging on to the slider and checking to see if it's done. The technique is just like  
with animations, except we'll make a slider reference instead of an animation reference.  
Add a new slider *reference right above the slide value line, and call it death fade slider.  
11 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Within the slider's block, add a new set *reference command and set the *death fade slider to the scoped  
^slider.  
Now, just as we've done with our animation references, add a new check value command to the death fade  
behavior to check if *death fade slider's > time remaining = 0.  
In the new check value block, when the slider completes, re-add the command to remove myself.  
Try running and seeing our fantastic, new slider in action. The enemy should fade out, then you'll see his prints go  
away when he's done fading out, indicating that he's been removed and shut down.  
You may have wondered: We're setting (sliding) the actor's tint. Why not just write a line in the death fade behavior  
that checks if his tint is 0, instead of doing the whole rigamaroll with the slider? You could do that, but a good  
implementation philosophy is to always rely upon the same data, whenever possible. For example, doing that would  
be analogous to playing an animation, then checking an attached object's position to query if the animation is done.  
In this case, we're starting a fade animation (a slider is like an animation), and we want to ask is this animation  
done? It's more direct, easier to read and maintain, and it's also safer. We'll get into the safety factors later.  
If you've been live updating and killing the enemy after adding the slider for his alpha, you may notice that when  
checking and unchecking the template box to bring him back, he comes back invisible! You'll see the spear  
swinging around as he attacks you, but the enemy's nowhere to be seen. To be fair, we're getting what we scripted -  
we slid his alpha to 0, and it stays 0. At this point, we're modifying the enemy so much that we need some way of  
bringing him back without using our little template checkbox hack.  
12 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
An Enemy Manager  
To accomplish our goal of having our enemy brought back to life when he dies, so we can test and improve upon  
him, we'll write an enemy manager. The enemy manager's job is to check the state of the enemy and return him to  
his original state if he dies.  
Using Control  
The easiest way to check if an object is active is to use the control command. Control has many diﬀerent  
implications, so let's list them out:  
If the object is inactive, activate it, and return true.  
If the object is active or the above occured, all commands and parameters with implicit context will now  
apply to the object that's being controlled. For example, have you noticed that there's no target when we play  
an animation? If you put a play inside a control, the animation would play on the controlled object.  
If the object is inactive and cannot be activated because he has been removed or is a template, the control will  
return false.  
Checking/Restoring Enemy's State  
In this case, the third feature is the one we are most interested in. We want to ﬁnd out if the player killed the  
enemy, so let's write a script that controls him, and, when it fails, restores the enemy after a brief delay.  
Make a new actor called Enemy Manager and create a new script for him and save it as EnemyManager.ai.  
Add a new behavior called check enemy, and, to it, add a new control command, and specify the Enemy  
Actor.  
Add an else statement to the control command, since we are interested in when the control fails.  
Create a new value variable and call it respawn delay.  
When the control fails, set the respawn delay to the Script Timer + 1, setting up a wait for one second.  
13 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Add a new behavior called respawn enemy and use set behavior to change to it when the control fails.  
In the respawn enemy behavior, add a new check value command to see if the Script Timer has equalled or  
surpassed our respawn delay.  
Once the delay is up, we need a way of restoring the enemy. When an actor has been removed, it can be restored to  
its normal state with the reset command. While reset works on many objects (and even variables!), the rule for  
objects is that it will force it to shutdown, if it hasn't already, and then will start it up again. It is primarily used as  
an inverse of the remove command, to un-remove it. Unfortunately, it will not restore the changed alpha value, so  
we will have to do that by hand.  
In the check value's block, add a new reset command and specify the Enemy Actor.  
Add a new set value command to set the Enemy Actor's > tint > alpha back to 255.  
Add a new set behavior to return to the check enemy behavior.  
The ﬁnished script should look at follows.  
14 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.3:_Intermediate...  
Build and launch, and check out your awesome enemy manager! When you kill the enemy, he'll fade out, then, 1  
second later, he should reappear at his origin. If you're having problems or want to verify it, add a print to target  
in the enemy manager to print its behavior.  
We could keep going down this road, but it annoys me that I have to make sure to set the enemy's tint when I  
restore him. It would be much nicer if I could just make him a template and then clone him, so the enemy can  
manipulate his own parameters without the manager having to worry about putting everything back the way it was.  
We'll be doing just that when you continue on to the fourth part of Intermediate Scripting.
